**Michael Collins** (Irish: *MÃ­cheÃ¡l Ã CoileÃ¡in*; 16 October 1890 â 22 August 1922) was an Irish revolutionary, soldier, and politician who was a leading figure in the early-20th-century struggle for Irish independence. Known by the nickname **"The Big Fellow,"** he served as Director of Intelligence of the Irish Republican Army (IRA) during the War of Independence, Minister for Finance of the self-declared Irish Republic, and Chairman of the Provisional Government of the Irish Free State until his assassination during the Irish Civil War.

### Early Life
Collins was born in **Woodfield, near Clonakilty, County Cork**, the youngest of eight children of Michael John Collins (a farmer and amateur mathematician) and Mary Anne O'Brien. His father died when Michael was just six and a half years old. He was a bright, precocious child with a fiery temper and a deep sense of Irish patriotism. Local figures â a blacksmith named James Santry and his schoolmaster Denis Lyons, a member of the Irish Republican Brotherhood (IRB) â first inspired his nationalist sentiments.

At age 15, Collins left school and moved to **London** in 1906 to work as a clerk in the Post Office Savings Bank. While in London, he joined the **London GAA** (Gaelic Athletic Association), through which he was introduced to the **Irish Republican Brotherhood (IRB)** by Sam Maguire. He also studied law at King's College London but did not complete his degree.

### Easter Rising (1916)
Collins returned to Ireland in January 1916 and participated in the **Easter Rising**, serving as aide-de-camp and bodyguard to Joseph Plunkett at the rebellion's headquarters in Dublin's **General Post Office (GPO)**. After the Rising's surrender, Collins was arrested and sent to **Frongoch internment camp** in Wales. The camp became a networking opportunity for republicans, and Collins emerged as a key organizer. He was released in December 1916 and immediately began planning for the next phase of the independence struggle.

Collins filled multiple roles during the War of Independence:
- **Director of Intelligence** of the IRA

He pioneered **guerrilla warfare** tactics against British forces, avoiding the large-scale, set-piece battles of the 1916 Rising. He created a specialized assassination unit known as **"The Squad"** to target British intelligence agents and informers. His most famous operation was **"Bloody Sunday"** on 21 November 1920, when Collins's Squad killed numerous British intelligence agents in coordinated raids across Dublin. The British responded with the **Black and Tans** and **Auxiliaries**, who waged a campaign of terror against Irish civilians.
